---
title: "Science Communication & Plain-Language Translation"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} · **Communication & Dissemination** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track](communication.qmd){.track-back}

*Turn dense findings into clear public-facing materials, at a reading level your audience can use.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Translate technical findings into plain language for a specific audience
- Control reading level, tone, and format for fact sheets, briefs, and social posts
- Check that simplification didn't distort the science

## Overview

AI is fast at turning a result into a brief, a fact sheet, or a social post — and at adjusting reading level on request. You decide the audience and confirm the simplified version is still accurate.

Plain language is an equity tool: clearer materials reach more people. But oversimplification can mislead, so you check that nothing important was lost or overstated.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Novice — Translate one finding for three audiences</summary>
```

**Time** ~18 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Turn a technical finding into clear materials for three different readers without distorting it.

**Setup.** Pick one public finding from your work, or use this example: *“In Philadelphia, pedestrian-involved crashes peak between 4–7 PM and are more common on wide arterial roads.”* Use public, non-sensitive content only.

**Steps.**

1. Paste the finding and ask for three versions:

   > Rewrite this public-health finding three ways: (a) for community members at an 8th-grade reading level, (b) for city policymakers, (c) as a social post under 280 characters. Keep every number accurate. [finding]

2. Check each version against the original — did any overstate the result or drop a caveat?

3. Push one further:

   > The community version is still a little technical. Simplify to a 6th-grade level, under 60 words, without losing the “4–7 PM” detail.

**Expected result.** Three audience-tailored versions, all factually faithful.

**Check your work.** Read the social post: could a reader screenshot it as a stronger claim than the data support? If so, tighten it.

**Common pitfalls.** Simplifying often turns “associated with” into “causes” — guard against it. Public data only.

**Stretch (optional).** Ask for a flyer-ready headline plus one supporting sentence.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Novice — Set and verify reading level</summary>
```

**Time** ~12 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu + a readability check

**Goal.** Deliberately set reading level and tone, then verify it independently.

**Setup.** A paragraph of your own writing (a non-sensitive report excerpt).

**Steps.**

1. Ask for a leveled rewrite:

   > Rewrite this at a 6th–8th grade reading level, warm and direct. Then tell me the approximate reading level of your rewrite and what you changed.

2. Verify with a real tool — paste into the Hemingway editor or check Word’s readability stats — and compare to the AI’s claim.

3. Get a contrasting version:

   > Now a more formal version for a stakeholder briefing, same facts.

**Expected result.** Two versions at different levels, plus a readability check that roughly matches.

**Check your work.** Don’t trust the AI’s self-reported grade level — confirm it with a real readability metric.

**Common pitfalls.** Plain language is not “dumbed down” — keep the accuracy. AI reading-level estimates are approximate.

**Stretch (optional).** Add a one-line “bottom line” a busy reader could act on.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "Plain-language rewriting is mainly an issue of…", "options": ["Equity — reaching more people", "Saving space", "Looking modern", "Avoiding citations"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Clearer materials are more accessible; that's the public-health value."}, {"q": "After AI simplifies a finding, you should check that…", "options": ["Nothing important was lost or overstated", "The font is larger", "It's shorter", "It uses synonyms"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Simplification can distort meaning — verify accuracy."}, {"q": "Which content is safe to use here?", "options": ["Public or non-sensitive findings", "Identifiable patient stories", "Unpublished embargoed results", "A private roster"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Keep practice and published material non-sensitive."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **Watch Your Tone: Language Machines as Writing Technology** — how AI shapes voice and clarity in writing.
- **A Framework for Technical Writing in the Age of LLMs (The Data Quarry)** — keeping accuracy while writing faster.

## Useful resources

- [plainlanguage.gov](https://www.plainlanguage.gov) — federal plain-language guidelines.
- [CDC Health Literacy](https://www.cdc.gov/healthliteracy/) — clear-communication guidance for health audiences.
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Use **public or non-sensitive** findings; never include identifiable patient details. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
